title: Can the Law Be Perfectly Obeyed? Are All Sins the Same? (WLC 149-150)
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy

outline.title: WLC 149: Can the Law Be Perfectly Obeyed?
subpoints: Answer: No man is able, either of himself or by any grace received in this life
Christians break the law daily in thought, word, and deed
The question: not just do you believe it, but can you defend it?

outline.title: Historical Error 1: Pelagianism
subpoints: Pelagius: God would not command what man cannot keep, therefore obedience is attainable
Augustine's rebuttal: all mankind fell in Adam; we are sinful by nature
Modern forms: 'people are born innocent,' 'everyone sins but most people are good by nature'
Ligonier survey: 65% of self-professed evangelicals say humans are born innocent
Reply: by what standard does Pelagius tell God what He can command? He ignores the fall.

outline.title: Historical Error 2: Christian Perfectionism
subpoints: Even a Christian cannot perfectly obey God's law in this life
Roman Catholicism, John Wesley, and the Higher Life movement taught otherwise
J.I. Packer was nearly undone by perfectionism as a young believer
Romans 7:21-23: Paul as a believer delights in God's law yet finds evil present
The Lord's Prayer implies daily sin — we must ask for forgiveness daily
Cannot hold perfectionism without a low view of the law

outline.title: WLC 150: Are All Sins Equally Heinous?
subpoints: Answer: Not equally heinous — some sins by reason of aggravations are more heinous
If all sins were the same + we cannot be perfect = growth in holiness is impossible
What is NOT being said: all sins are still heinous; none are acceptable
Clarification: the smallest sin still deserves God's eternal wrath
Scriptural support: Jesus distinguishes levels of judgment (Matthew 11:21-23)
Jesus distinguishes anger from murder, not equating them but showing seed-form sin
John 19:11: Jesus says Judas has the 'greater sin'
1 John 5:16: sins that lead to death vs. those that do not
Psalm 19:12-13: secret faults vs. presumptuous sins
1 Corinthians 5: Paul calls for immediate excommunication for a specific sin but not others

summary: Elder Andrew Hoy concludes the Westminster Larger Catechism Sunday School series by addressing questions 149 and 150, asking whether the law can be perfectly obeyed and whether all sins are equally heinous. For WLC 149, he takes a historical approach, examining two departures from orthodox teaching. First, Pelagianism — the teaching of Augustine's fourth-century opponent Pelagius, who argued that since God commands obedience, man must be capable of it. Hoy refutes this with the Scriptural testimony of the fall and the universal sinfulness of mankind, and notes that similar ideas appear in a majority of self-professed evangelicals today. Second, Christian perfectionism — the idea that believers can attain sinless perfection in this life, taught by Wesley and the Higher Life movement. Hoy refutes this with Romans 7 and the Lord's Prayer, arguing that perfectionism inevitably requires a low view of the law's demanding standard. For WLC 150, he establishes that not all sins are equally heinous, showing this is not a license for sin but a tool for fighting it. He provides scriptural support from Jesus's Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 11, John 19:11, 1 John 5:16, Psalm 19, and 1 Corinthians 5. The lesson ends with encouragement for the scrupulous Christian: those truly in Christ are clothed in His righteousness, freed from slavery to sin, and called to a lifelong battle of sanctification that is itself a gift of God's grace.
transcript:
## Introduction

All right, we are at our final lesson on the 10 commandments based on the Westminster Larger Catechism. This morning I will be addressing questions 149 through 151, which look back on the commandments and ask some key questions about how we think about the law of God.

Before we get into that, let's pray. Gracious God, we thank you for your word. We thank you that you are a God who has not left us to ourselves but has revealed yourself. And in revealing yourself, you have shown us your holy law, your holy standards for us. I pray as we conclude this series that we would be encouraged, strengthened, and stirred toward greater holiness in this life. We pray this all in Jesus' precious name. Amen.

I did my best to trim this down because it was already fairly substantial. So like I said, these questions aren't commentary on the 10 commandments themselves, but rather they look back at the commandments, asking some further questions. The first question we'll consider is: can these commandments be obeyed perfectly? The follow-up to that is: if not, does that mean that all sins are the same?

## WLC 149: Can the Law Be Perfectly Obeyed?

Larger Catechism question 149 asks: Is any man able perfectly to keep the commands of God? Answer: No man is able either of himself or by any grace received in this life perfectly to keep the commandments of God, but does daily break them in thought, word, and deed.

A fairly straightforward question and answer. No one can perfectly obey God's law — not even believers. My sense is that most of us in this room won't have many objections to this. My question for everyone is not just whether you believe it, but whether you can defend it. If you met someone who believed that perfection was attainable in this life, would you know where to go in Scripture to address that?

I'm going to take a historical approach by looking at two historical errors — one a heresy, one a great error — and then trace them through to contemporary times.

### Error 1: Pelagianism

Throughout church history, not everyone who bore the name of Christian has believed it's impossible for men to perfectly obey God's law. The most prominent name among those who thought perfection attainable would be Pelagius. His name often comes up in the context of his debates with one of the greatest theologians of church history, Augustine of Hippo.

Augustine held that all mankind fell in Adam — we are sinful by nature and unable to overcome sin in our own strength. Whereas Pelagius didn't believe that Adam's sin impacted his posterity. According to Pelagius, humanity is born neutral; we sin by choice, and we can choose not to sin.

My reason for bringing up Pelagius is specifically because of how his argument ties to the Ten Commandments. Since he believed people could choose not to sin, he had a biblical argument — at least in his own mind. It was this: God would not command what man cannot keep. So when God commands "You shall have no other gods before me," it must be possible on your own strength to worship God truly. Likewise with "You shall not covet" — that should be within our own strength. And since Jesus says in Matthew 5:48 "You shall be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect," Pelagius concluded that humanity must be capable of becoming not just holy but perfect — by nature, not by the Spirit.

How would you reply to Pelagius? First, on what basis does any man tell God how He must run His world? By telling God what He can and cannot command, Pelagius is putting himself in the place of God. Second, Pelagius ignores the estate of fallen mankind — we would confess that Adam was able to keep the law, but that humanity has fallen.

Do you know any card-carrying Pelagians? Probably not. But you likely know people who espouse similar ideas. How about: "Humans are neutral — we're born innocent, not in sin." Or: "People aren't that bad, they just do bad things." Or — and this is one we might slip into ourselves — looking at an unbelieving friend who is kind, generous, and disciplined and thinking: "Surely God couldn't have a problem with him."

The Ligonier State of Theology survey found that among self-professed evangelicals, 65% said yes when asked whether humans are born innocent. And even more agreed with the statement that everyone sins a little but most people are good by nature. These ideas are more prevalent than we might think.

### Error 2: Christian Perfectionism

So Pelagius was speaking of humanity in general. What about Christians? Can a believer, possessed of the Spirit of God, attain perfect obedience in this life? Our catechism says no — "or by any grace received in this life." And it goes further: even Christians daily break God's law in thought, word, and deed.

Have all Christians believed this? No. The Roman Catholic Church teaches that perfect obedience is attainable. Within Protestantism, Christian perfectionism was most popularly taught by John Wesley, the great evangelist of the First Great Awakening. There are also famous anecdotal stories of Charles Spurgeon testing the holiness of a self-professed perfectionist — in various versions of the story, by throwing a glass of milk, a mug of beer, or a cup of hot tea on them to see how they'd respond.

In the late 1800s and early 1900s there was a Higher Life movement based on the Keswick conventions in England. That movement gave us the phrase "let go and let God." J.I. Packer was swept up in it as a young believer and said it nearly did him in — the burden of being taught that perfect obedience was attainable was crushing.

Now, to give credit where it's due: the men who taught Christian perfectionism were often godly men who took seriously the high call to holiness in Scripture. They knew passages like 1 Peter 1:16 ("Be holy as God is holy"), Matthew 5:48 ("Be perfect as your Father is perfect"), and 1 Corinthians 10:13 ("No temptation has overtaken you except such as is common to man"). Their desire for holiness was admirable.

But where they go wrong: Romans 7 is perhaps the most helpful passage. Those who subscribe to Christian perfection read Romans 7 as the pre-conversion Paul. But it's very hard to believe this is Paul before he was a Christian, because Paul says in verse 22: "I delight in the law of God according to the inward man." Does an unbeliever delight in the law of God? The Psalms reserve that language for the godly man. Paul as a believer, one who delights in God's law, continued to battle indwelling sin.

Another refutation: the Lord's Prayer. It is a model prayer for daily Christian use. And what does it instruct us to pray daily? "Forgive us our debts." If we are supposed to daily ask for forgiveness, that implies we are sinning daily. The only way to hold to Christian perfectionism is to have a very low view of what God's law actually demands. I hope that anyone who sat through the last few months of this series on the Ten Commandments — and saw how extensive the catechism's unpacking of those commandments is — would find it astounding to walk away thinking, "Yes, I've attained that."

## WLC 150: Are All Sins Equally Heinous?

If Christians continue to sin and cannot attain perfection, two questions press on us: (1) Can I make any real progress in holiness? And (2) What makes Christians different from unbelievers? The catechism answers both by asking: are all sins the same?

Question 150: Are all transgressions of the law of God equally heinous in themselves and in the sight of God? Answer: All transgressions of the law of God are not equally heinous, but some sins in themselves and by reason of several aggravations are more heinous in the sight of God than others.

My hunch is that if you surveyed a large group of evangelicals, perhaps 95% would say all sins are the same. This seems to be the default position of many Christians today.

### What Is at Stake?

If all sins are the same and we cannot keep the law perfectly, then growth in holiness is effectively impossible. Because if you continue to sin every day and all sins are the same, progress means nothing — you'd just be swapping out old sins for new ones. And you'd also have to say there is no practical difference between believers and unbelievers.

But if some sins are more heinous than others, that changes everything — how we understand sanctification, how we think about our own holiness and progress.

### What Is NOT Being Said

When the catechism says not all sins are equally heinous, it is NOT saying that some sins are acceptable. The word "heinous" is the key: all sins are heinous — terrible, abominable, really, really bad. The catechism simply recognizes that some are more heinous than others. The smallest sin still deserves the wrath and curse of God. Even eating a piece of fruit that you were told not to eat brought devastating consequences.

### Scriptural Support

The goal is not to create "respectable sins" or a lower standard we can attain — it is to understand our enemy better so we can fight it better.

First, Jesus misunderstood. Some people read Matthew 5:27-28 — "whoever looks at a woman to lust for her has already committed adultery with her in his heart" — and conclude that all sins are the same. But Jesus doesn't say the lust is the same as acting out adultery. He says he has committed adultery "in his heart." He's showing that the heart sin is real and heinous — it is the seed form of the outward sin. Acting out adultery begins with that seed in the heart.

In Matthew 5:21-22, Jesus addresses anger: "You have heard it was said... you shall not murder. But I say to you, whoever is angry with his brother without cause shall be in danger of the judgment. Whoever says to his brother 'Raca' shall be in danger of the council, and whoever says 'You fool' shall be in danger of hellfire." Notice that even within anger, Jesus makes distinctions between levels of judgment for different offenses.

Matthew 11:21-23: Jesus pronounces woe on Chorazin and Bethsaida. "It will be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon in the day of judgment than for you. And you, Capernaum... it will be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment than for you." Jesus is making a distinction in judgment — implying different levels of sin.

John 19:11: Jesus says to Pilate, "Therefore the one who delivered me to you has the greater sin" — explicit language of distinction between sins.

1 John 5:16: John speaks of "sins which lead to death" and those which do not, indicating there is a category of sin for which repentance is not possible.

Psalm 19:12-13: David prays, "Who can understand his errors? Cleanse me from secret faults. Keep back your servant also from presumptuous sins." David recognizes at least two categories: secret sins (general, daily failures he's not even fully aware of) and presumptuous sins (overt, intentional violations of God's law).

Treatment of sins: 1 Corinthians 5 — Paul calls for immediate excommunication for the man openly living in an adulterous relationship with his stepmother. But in the same letter he addresses many other sins — quarreling, lawsuits, confusion about marriage, party spirit, eating food offered to idols — without the same extreme reaction. That is Paul treating different sins differently.

The Old Testament law also assigns different punishments to different transgressions — some require a sacrifice, some require restitution, some require exclusion from the camp, and some carry the death penalty.

### Closing Encouragement

For the person who is striving for righteousness, who examines their life with a microscope and feels weighed down by all the remaining sin — I would first commend their desire to honor God. Then I would remind them of six things that are true of every Christian:

1. If you are truly in Christ, you are clothed in the righteousness of Christ — God does not see your sins; He sees you as perfect as His Son.
2. If you are truly in Christ, you are no longer a slave to sin.
3. If you are truly in Christ, you will not persist in unrepentant sin.
4. If you are truly in Christ, you will battle and fight against sin and temptation until the day you die — sanctification is a lifelong battle.
5. Fighting sin requires serious effort. Don't expect an easy road.
6. The fight against sin is itself a gift of God's grace.

As 1 John says: "If we say we are without sin, we deceive ourselves." Yet: "If we confess our sin, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness."

Let's pray. Gracious and glorious God, we thank you that in your Son Jesus Christ our sins are not held against us, that we are declared righteous. I pray that each person in this room that knows Christ as Lord and Savior would remember that their hope is in Him. Their hope is not in their ability to follow your law. Their only hope is in Jesus Christ. But as they know their hope is in Christ alone, may that drive them to fight against sin — because by being declared righteous in Christ they have a calling to then live out that righteousness in their own life. May we be strengthened this morning in the preaching of your word. In Jesus' precious name, amen.
